label: Defamation
scenario_patterns: A social media post accuses a business owner of fraud without factual basis
A newspaper publishes an article falsely alleging a politician accepted bribes
An ex-employer provides a defamatory reference to a prospective employer
An online forum post falsely accuses a hawker stall of using unsanitary ingredients
legal_tests: The statement must be defamatory — it must tend to lower the plaintiff in the estimation of right-thinking members of society, cause the plaintiff to be shunned or avoided, or expose the plaintiff to hatred, contempt, or ridicule
The statement must refer to the plaintiff (directly or by reasonable inference)
The statement must be published — communicated to at least one person other than the plaintiff
Libel (written/permanent form) is actionable per se; slander (spoken/transient form) generally requires proof of special damage unless it falls within recognised exceptions
The natural and ordinary meaning or innuendo meaning of the words is assessed from the perspective of an ordinary reasonable reader
common_fact_patterns: Online defamation via social media platforms, blogs, or forum posts
Defamation by newspapers, magazines, or broadcast media
Corporate defamation — false statements about a company's business practices
Workplace defamation — false statements by employers or colleagues affecting reputation
Political defamation — false accusations against public figures or politicians
sg_case_references: Lee Hsien Loong v Roy Ngerng Yi Ling [2014] SGHC 230 — online defamation, assessment of damages for defamatory blog posts
Review Publishing Co Ltd v Lee Hsien Loong [2010] SGCA 1 — Reynolds privilege (responsible journalism) considered and not adopted in Singapore
Lim Eng Hock Peter v Lin Jian Wei [2010] SGCA 26 — defamation damages and the natural and ordinary meaning test
Golden Season Pte Ltd v Kairos Singapore Holdings Pte Ltd [2015] SGHC 38 — corporate defamation and publication on online platforms
Lee Kuan Yew v Vinocur [1995] SGHC 170 — defamation by foreign media concerning a Singapore public figure
party_roles: plaintiff/claimant whose reputation is allegedly damaged
original source of the defamatory allegation
defences: Justification (truth) — the statement is substantially true in its defamatory meaning
Fair comment — honest opinion on a matter of public interest based on true facts
Absolute privilege — statements made in parliamentary or judicial proceedings
Qualified privilege — statements made on an occasion of duty or interest, without malice
Offer of amends under the Defamation Act (Cap 75) — prompt correction and apology
